topic: Prepositional pronouns, question words, and adverbs

tips.title: Mí wears an accent; mi does not
tips.body: Mí (me, used after a preposition, like 'para mí') and mi (my, from Day 11) look nearly identical, but the accent mark changes everything. It's a perfect example of how one tiny mark can flip a word's whole job in Spanish.

tips.title: Conmigo and contigo are permanent fusions
tips.body: Spanish never says 'con mí' or 'con ti' -- centuries ago those combinations welded into single words: conmigo (with me) and contigo (with you). Just memorize them as their own vocabulary words.

tips.title: Sí and si are another accent trap
tips.body: Sí with an accent means 'yes.' Si without an accent (from Day 13) means 'if.' Same letters, same sound, completely different jobs -- only the written accent and the surrounding sentence tell them apart.

tips.title: Every question word carries a written accent
tips.body: Quién, dónde, cómo -- every single interrogative word in Spanish has an accent on its stressed syllable, even when it's tucked inside a statement like 'no sé dónde está' (I don't know where it is).

bonusCards.english: Do you want to go with me, or do you want to go with her?
How are you? I am very well.
Where is my friend now? He is only with me, like this.
I never go with him; I only go with you.
Who has eaten already? Only my friend has eaten.

resources.name: Kwiziq Spanish — Sí and Si (with or without accent)
